back_translation: When all of us had separated from them and set out by ship, we sailed straight to the island of Cos, the next day to the island of Rhodes, and from there to the city of Patara.
There all of us found a certain ship which was about to cross the sea to the province of Phoenicia, so we boarded that ship and continued the voyage.

When we sighted the island of Cyprus, all of us sailed past it on the southern side, heading for the province of Syria, and we landed at the city of Tyre, because that ship was going to unload its cargo there.
All of us sought out and found the disciples there and stayed with them seven days. They warned Paul repeatedly by the Spirit not to go to Jerusalem.

When all of us had stayed there the full number of days, we set out on the journey, with all the believers along with their wives and their children coming out to see us off until beyond the city limits, and we knelt down on the beach and prayed together.
When we had said farewell to one another, all of us boarded the ship, while they returned to their own homes.
When all of us had completed the voyage from the city of Tyre, we arrived at the city of Ptolemais, and we greeted the brothers there and stayed with them for one day.
The next day all of us continued the journey on to the city of Caesarea, and we stayed at the home of Philip the proclaimer-of-the-good-news, who was one of those seven men.
Philip had four daughters who were still virgins, and every one of them spoke prophecy.
While all of us were staying there many days, a certain prophet named Agabus came down from the province of Judea.

He came to all of us, took Paul's belt, bound his own feet and hands with it, and said, 'The Holy Spirit speaks thus: "The Jews in Jerusalem will bind the owner of this belt in this manner, and will hand him over into the hands of the Gentiles."'
When all of us and the believers in that city heard such a word, we joined together in pleading with Paul with tears not to go up to Jerusalem.
Paul therefore answered, 'Why are all of you weeping and making my heart shatter? For I am ready — not only to be bound, but to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus as well.'
When he would not accept the persuasion, all of us became silent and said, 'May the will of the Lord be fulfilled.'
After a few days, all of us prepared our baggage and traveled up to Jerusalem.
Some of the disciples from the city of Caesarea also traveled together with us, leading all of us to stay at the home of Mnason, a Cypriot, who was a disciple from the early period.
When all of us arrived at Jerusalem, the brothers there welcomed us with joy.
The next day Paul, together with all of us, went in to meet James, and every one of the church-elders also came to the meeting.
Paul greeted them and recounted one matter at a time about the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his service.

When they heard, they offered honor to God, then said to him, 'Brother, you have seen that among the Jews there are tens of thousands of people who have believed, and every one of them is one who is strict toward the Law.

But someone has told them about you that you teach all the Jews who live among the Gentiles to forsake the Law of Moses, by telling them not to circumcise their own children or to walk according to the customs.
If so, what should we do? They will certainly hear that you have come.
Therefore, please do as we advise: there are four men among us who have made a vow of their own.

Please take these four men and join in the rite of purification together with them, and pay the expenses on their behalf so that they may shave their heads. Then everyone will know that what they have heard about you is not true, but that you yourself live as one who keeps the Law.

But concerning the Gentiles who have already believed, we have written and sent a letter to inform them of our decision that they must abstain from things sacrificed to idols, from blood, from meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality.'

The next day, therefore, Paul took those four men, joined in the rite of purification together with them, and entered the area of the temple to give notice of the day of completion of the rite of purification, until an offering would be presented for each one of them.
When the seven days were almost completed, certain Jews from the province of Asia saw Paul in the area of the temple, so they incited the entire crowd and arrested him.

They shouted, 'Men of Israel, please give help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere to oppose our people, to oppose the Law, and to oppose this place. Moreover, he has also brought Greeks into the area of the temple and has defiled this holy place as well.'
For previously they had seen Trophimus the Ephesian with him in the city, so they assumed on their own that Paul had brought him into the area of the temple as well.
The whole city was thrown into turmoil; the crowd rushed in to lynch him; they grabbed Paul and dragged him outside the area of the temple, and the gates were shut at once.
While the crowd was seeking a way to kill him, a report went up to the commander of the Roman troop-unit that all of Jerusalem was in turmoil.
The commander therefore took soldiers and centurions and rushed down to the crowd at once. When the crowd saw the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
When the commander came near, he seized Paul and ordered that two chains be used to bind him, and then questioned the crowd as to who he was and what he had done.

Among the crowd, some shouted one thing, others shouted another. When the commander could not get at the truth because of the crowd's tumult, he ordered that Paul be brought into the military barracks.
When he reached the steps, the soldiers had to help carry him up because of the violence of the crowd.
For the crowd kept following and shouting, 'Get rid of him!'
While the soldiers were about to take Paul into the barracks, he said to the commander, 'May I make a brief request to speak with you?' The commander answered, 'Are you able to speak Greek?'
Are you not that Egyptian man who previously incited a rebellion and led four thousand of the Sicarii out into the wilderness?'
Paul answered, 'I am a Jew, a citizen of the city of Tarsus in the province of Cilicia, a citizen of a city that is not ordinary. I beseech you, please permit me to speak to the people.'
When the commander permitted, Paul stood on the steps and waved his hand to the people. When everyone became silent, he spoke to them in the Hebrew language, saying:
